metadata.summary: Object creation patterns (Factory, Builder, DI, Object Pool, Prototype, Abstract Factory, Singleton) decoupling creation from usage for testable code.

content.title: Creational Design Patterns
content.summary: Object creation mechanisms that increase flexibility and reuse. Factory Method for runtime type selection, Builder for complex multi-step construction, Dependency Injection for loose coupling, Object Pool for expensive resource reuse, Prototype for cloning, Abstract Factory for product families, Singleton for process-global resources (use sparingly).

why.p: Choosing the right creational pattern directly affects testability, maintainability and refactoring speed. Dependency Injection reduces maintenance bugs by ~27%, while overuse of Singleton makes testing ~40% harder due to hidden global state. Each pattern targets a specific creation problem at a different complexity level — picking the wrong one creates churn, picking the right one removes whole categories of bugs.

list.item: Writing object-creation code where the concrete type is decided at runtime (e.g. payment provider by country, storage backend by config).
Constructor has 4+ parameters or many optional params — Builder beats telescoping constructors.
Refactoring code with duplicated `new X(...)` chains spread across modules — centralize via Factory.
Reviewing legacy code for Singleton overuse and proposing DI migrations.
Writing test fixtures and mocks — DI and Factory Method dramatically reduce test scaffolding.
Working with expensive-to-create resources (DB connections, HTTP clients, threads) — Object Pool.
Trivial constructors with 1-3 params — `dataclass` / record / struct is enough.
Cross-cutting infra concerns where DI containers already exist (FastAPI Depends, NestJS providers, Spring beans).
One-off scripts where pattern overhead exceeds the value.
High-performance hot paths where DI lookup cost matters (resolve once, hold reference).
Singleton for anything that holds mutable state, has dependencies, or differs between tests.

rule.statement: Default to Dependency Injection for any component with dependencies. Never reach for Singleton unless the dependency is truly process-global, read-only, and stateless.
rule.rationale: DI reduces maintenance bugs by ~27%, improves testability (70% of high-performing teams rely on mocking), enables modularity, and accelerates refactoring. DI frameworks exist in every mainstream language.

rule.statement: Use Builder when a constructor has more than 3 parameters or any optional parameter, instead of overloading constructors or passing positional `None`/`null` placeholders.
rule.rationale: Telescoping constructors are unreadable and break call-site refactoring. Builders make required vs optional explicit and produce immutable results.

rule.statement: Use Factory Method when the concrete subclass to instantiate depends on runtime input (config, env, request payload). Do not litter `if/elif` constructor switches across business logic.
rule.rationale: Centralizing the switch in one factory keeps domain code closed against new variants and makes it possible to add a new variant by registering it once.

list.item: One concrete class, simple constructor → no pattern, plain constructor or `dataclass`.
One interface, several concrete impls chosen at runtime → Factory Method.
Several related families of products (e.g. UI kit per OS, DB driver per vendor) → Abstract Factory.
Complex object built step-by-step with optional fields → Builder.
Component needs collaborators it does not own → Dependency Injection (constructor injection preferred).
Expensive resource reused many times → Object Pool with explicit acquire/release.
Need many similar objects derived from a configured original → Prototype (clone).
Truly process-global, read-only, stateless utility → Singleton (last resort, prefer module-level immutable).

how-to.step: Identify the creation pain: count constructor params, count `if`/`switch` on type, list expensive-resource creations, list global state. Capture as a 5-line note in the file you are editing.
Match the pain to ONE pattern using the decision tree above. Write the choice + reason as an ADR-style 3-line comment at the top of the new factory/builder/container module.
Define the abstraction first: protocol/interface for the product, signature for `create(...)` or `build()`. Avoid leaking concrete types into callers.
Implement the chosen pattern in a single dedicated file (`factory.py`, `builder.ts`, `pool.go`). Keep it under 150 LOC; if longer, split product variants into one file each.

Wire callers: replace `new X(...)` with `factory.create(kind)` / `Builder().with_x(...).build()` / `container.resolve(IService)`. Use editor refactor "Find references" to ensure no direct constructor leak remains.
Add unit tests that swap implementations via the same abstraction (mock factory, fake DI container, stub product). Tests prove decoupling actually works.
For Object Pool: implement explicit `acquire`/`release`, set `min`/`max`/`idle_timeout`, add a healthcheck before handing out; never let the pool grow unbounded.
For Singleton: justify in a comment why it is not DI, mark fields immutable (`Final`, `readonly`, `const`), and provide a test-only `_reset_for_tests()` hook.

checklist.check: Pain identified and written down (params count, type-switch count, resource cost, global state).
Exactly ONE pattern selected from the decision tree; ADR-style note added at top of module.
No `if isinstance` / `switch (type)` chains remaining in caller code after refactor.
No constructor of a concrete product called outside the factory/builder/container.
Builder produces immutable result; required fields validated in `build()`.
DI uses constructor injection; no service locator inside business logic.
Object Pool has bounded size, idle timeout, healthcheck, and explicit release in `finally`/`defer`/`using`.
Singleton (if used) is read-only, stateless, has `_reset_for_tests`, and a comment explaining why DI was rejected.
Unit tests substitute implementations via the abstraction (mock/fake/stub) without monkey-patching globals.
Linter passes (`ruff check . --fix` or `eslint --fix .`); type-checker passes (`mypy` / `tsc --noEmit`).
No new circular imports introduced (run `pydeps` or check IDE error panel).

commands.command: pytest tests/ -k "factory or builder or container or pool" -q

example.title: Factory Method (Python) — payment provider per country
example.note: Adding a new provider = one registry line, no caller change. Tests inject a `FakeProvider` by mutating a copy of the registry per-test.

example.title: Builder (TypeScript) — query construction
Dependency Injection (Python, FastAPI)
Singleton hiding state — anti-pattern

antipattern.description: Service Locator inside business logic: `Container.resolve(EmailService)` called from a domain method.
antipattern.reason: Hides dependencies, defeats DI, and turns tests back into integration tests. Inject through the constructor instead.

antipattern.description: Builder that returns a mutable object with public setters after `build()`.
antipattern.reason: Defeats the immutability guarantee. Either freeze the result or expose only getters.

antipattern.description: Factory that takes the concrete class as an argument: `factory.create(StripeProvider)`.
antipattern.reason: Caller already knows the concrete type — the factory adds zero value. Dispatch on a string/enum or inject the class once at config time.

template.purpose: Registry-based factory skeleton — copy and rename

content.prompt:
You are reviewing a creational-pattern refactor. Inputs: - the diff (paste below) - the call sites grep output (paste below)
Tasks: 1. State which creational pattern was applied and why (one sentence). 2. Verify the decision against this tree: - one impl, simple ctor → none - runtime type selection → Factory Method - product families → Abstract Factory - >3 params or optional → Builder - external collaborators → Dependency Injection - expensive resource reuse → Object Pool - clone-from-original → Prototype - process-global read-only → Singleton (last resort) 3. List every direct concrete constructor that still escapes the abstraction. 4. List every Singleton with mutable state or hidden IO. 5. Output a numbered FIX list (each item: file:line + concrete change). Reply only in markdown, no prose preface.

verify.step: Run the relevant lint/type commands from the `commands` block; all must exit 0.
Run pattern-focused tests: `pytest -k "factory or builder or container or pool"` (or jest equivalent). All must pass.
Grep for leaks: `find-direct-constructors` and `find-singletons-py` / `find-singletons-ts` must return zero unexpected matches outside factory/test files.
Confirm the decision note (ADR-style 3 lines) exists at the top of the new pattern module.
Confirm at least one test substitutes the implementation through the abstraction (mock/fake/stub) without monkey-patching globals.
For Object Pool: stress-test acquire/release for `2 * max_size` iterations and assert no leak (final pool size == max_size, idle workers reaped).
For Singleton: assert `_reset_for_tests()` exists and is called between unit tests via fixture.
Update CHANGELOG.md `[Unreleased]` with a one-line entry describing the refactor.

content.warning: Do not introduce a DI container into a project that does not have one; constructor injection by hand is enough for solo-tier projects. A container is justified only when 10+ services share a graph.
